title: Product BOM Decomposition: GfS Türwächter IoT

sections.title: Market Context: Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions (NAICS 712110)
sections.content:
*Follows BOM Decomposition component spec (B.1–B.4). Product is a manufactured physical device — BOM applies.*
*Source NAICS for manufacturing: 334290 / 335999 (Security & Communications Equipment). Target market: 712110.*

---

sections.title: NAICS Routing Check
sections.content:
| Criterion | Value |
|---|---|
| Product NAICS (manufacturer) | 334290 — Communications Equipment / Security Devices |
| Target market NAICS | 712110 — Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions |
| BOM applicable? | **YES** — product is a manufactured physical device with compositional hierarchy |
| Target market notes | Service sector (71); BOM is of the product, not the museum's operations |

---

sections.title: Output Type (L5)
sections.content:
**Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT** — Emergency exit door alarm guard with 868 MHz wireless state reporting. Surface-mounted on door handle. Mechanically alarmed on 45° guard swing. Battery-powered IoT module supervisory only.

Museum-specific context: Heritage buildings restrict structural modification. Non-invasive surface mounting and battery operation (no mains wiring) are the core product-market fit drivers.

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
| L4 Subsystem | Function | Museum relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **MECH** — Mechanical Locking Subsystem | Physically blocks handle depression; releases on 45° guard swing; triggers alarm linkage | Primary function; must not damage historic door hardware |
| **ALARM** — Acoustic Alarm Subsystem | Generates 95 dB alarm on guard activation; operates without electrical power | No power dependency critical for heritage buildings with unreliable power infrastructure |
| **IOT** — IoT Monitoring Subsystem | Wireless state broadcast (guard open / alarm / battery low) to receiver; battery power management | Protocol lock-in (EasyWave) creates integration friction with museum security/CCTV systems |
| **ENC** — Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem | Outer housing; surface-mount bracket; installation hardware | Non-damaging mount to heritage door fixtures; colour/finish may need to match interior aesthetics |

---

sections.title: B.2 + B.3 — Full Decomposition with Alternatives
sections.content:
### L4-MECH — Mechanical Locking Subsystem

```
L4  Mechanical Locking Subsystem
 ├── L3  Guard Actuator Assembly
 │    ├── L2  Guard Body & Pivot Assembly
 │    │    ├── L1  Guard housing body (injection molded)
 │    │    ├── L1  Pivot axle
 │    │    └── L1  Pivot bearing / bushing
 │    └── L2  Return Spring Assembly
 │         ├── L1  Torsion spring
 │         └── L0  Spring steel wire (EN 10270-1)
 ├── L3  Handle Engagement Mechanism
 │    ├── L2  Handle clamp assembly
 │    │    ├── L1  Clamp block
 │    │    └── L0  Engineering polymer (PA66) or zinc die-cast
 │    └── L2  Trigger linkage
 │         ├── L1  Cam element
 │         └── L0  Steel / zinc alloy
 └── L3  Key Override Assembly
      ├── L2  Half-cylinder housing
      │    ├── L1  Cylinder body
      │    └── L0  Brass (CuZn39Pb3)
      └── L2  Key bitting mechanism
           └── L1  Pins, springs, plug
```

#### Alternatives at L4-MECH level — Guard protection principle

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Guard-over-handle (Türwächter-style) | 45% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — dominant retrofit category |
| Alarmed push bar (integrated, e.g. GfS e-Bar, dormakaba) | 30% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate |
| Electronic exit control with panic release (wired) | 20% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — smart building trend |
| Magnetic contact + separate alarm | 5% | v Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate |

**Museum note:** Guard-over-handle is preferred for heritage contexts — installs on existing handle without door modification. Electronic wired systems face heritage building constraints (no cable runs through historic walls).

#### Alternatives at L3 — Key Override mechanism

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Profile half-cylinder key | 70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — standard in EU security hardware |
| Code keypad override | 20% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate |
| RFID / NFC card override | 10% | ^ Growing | LOW | Analyst estimate |

**Museum note:** RFID override aligns with museum staff access card systems (many museums use RFID for staff). Growing integration opportunity.

#### Alternatives at L0 — Clamp body material

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glass-filled polyamide PA66-GF | 40% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Industry material selection practice |
| Zinc die-cast (Zamak) | 35% | Stable | MEDIUM | Traditional hardware die-casting |
| ABS/PC blend | 20% | Stable | MEDIUM | Lower-cost variant |
| Stainless steel (CNC machined) | 5% | Stable | LOW | Premium / IP-rated variants |

---

### L4-ALARM — Acoustic Alarm Subsystem

```
L4  Acoustic Alarm Subsystem
 ├── L3  Piezoelectric Alarm Module
 │    ├── L2  Piezo Transducer Assembly
 │    │    ├── L1  Piezo disc (active element)
 │    │    ├── L0  PZT ceramic (lead zirconate titanate)
 │    │    ├── L1  Metal diaphragm
 │    │    └── L0  Brass alloy (CuZn28)
 │    └── L2  Resonator housing
 │         ├── L1  Horn body / resonator cavity
 │         └── L0  ABS thermoplastic
 └── L3  Pre-Alarm Trigger Assembly (optional variant only)
      ├── L2  Vibration sensing sub-assembly
      │    ├── L1  Micro-switch or capacitive touch sensor
      │    └── L1  PCB carrier
      └── L0  FR4 laminate substrate
```

#### Alternatives at L3 — Alarm transducer type

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric buzzer | 75% | Stable | MEDIUM | [Straits Research — Buzzer Market](https://straitsresearch.com/report/buzzer-market); dominant for low-power IoT/security |
| Electromagnetic buzzer | 20% | v Declining | MEDIUM | Legacy — higher power draw, moving parts |
| Electrodynamic speaker | 5% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate — used in premium multi-tone devices |

**Museum note:** Piezoelectric is load-bearing here — operates mechanically without electrical power. This is a non-negotiable for regulatory compliance (EltVTR). No realistic alternative at this level for the Türwächter's regulatory use case.

#### Alternatives at L0 — Resonator housing material

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS | 55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Standard injection-molded enclosure material |
| ABS/PC blend | 30% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Better impact resistance |
| PA6 (polyamide) | 10% | Stable | LOW | Where heat resistance needed |
| UV-stabilized ASA | 5% | ^ Emerging | LOW | Outdoor / UV-exposed variants |

---

### L4-IOT — IoT Monitoring Subsystem

```
L4  IoT Monitoring Subsystem
 ├── L3  RF Transmitter Module
 │    ├── L2  RF Circuit Board Assembly
 │    │    ├── L1  RF transceiver IC (868 MHz)
 │    │    ├── L1  Crystal oscillator
 │    │    ├── L1  Passive components (capacitors, resistors, inductors)
 │    │    └── L0  FR4 PCB laminate / copper foil
 │    └── L2  Antenna Assembly
 │         ├── L1  PCB trace antenna or helical element
 │         └── L0  Copper conductor
 ├── L3  Power Management Module
 │    ├── L2  Battery Holder Assembly
 │    │    ├── L1  Spring contact clips
 │    │    └── L0  Phosphor bronze / stainless steel
 │    └── L2  Power regulation circuit
 │         ├── L1  LDO voltage regulator IC
 │         ├── L1  Decoupling capacitors
 │         └── L0  Silicon, ceramic, tantalum
 └── L3  State Detection Module
      ├── L2  Position sensor assembly
      │    ├── L1  Reed switch or Hall-effect sensor
      │    └── L0  Soft magnetic alloy (Fe-Ni)
      └── L2  Status indicator assembly
           ├── L1  LED
           └── L0  GaAs/InGaN semiconductor
```

#### Alternatives at L3 — Wireless radio protocol (most critical design choice)

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proprietary 868 MHz (EasyWave / Z-Wave / KEELOQ-based) | 35% | v Declining | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — large EU installed base; no new platform growth |
| Zigbee (2.4 GHz mesh) | 25% | Stable | MEDIUM | [IHS Markit Connectivity Report](https://cdn.ihs.com/www/pdf/IHS%20Markit%20-%20Connectivity%20Technology%20Competition%20Drivers%20and%20Influencers.pdf); dominant in smart building mesh |
| BLE 5.x (Bluetooth Low Energy) | 20% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | [Bluetooth.com IoT comparison](https://www.bluetooth.com/blog/wireless-connectivity-options-for-iot-applications-technology-comparison/) |
| LoRaWAN (868 MHz LPWAN) | 10% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | [GM Insights — LoRaWAN Market 2025–2034, CAGR 41%](https://www.gminsights.com/industry-analysis/lorawan-market); strong for large-building / campus deployments |
| Z-Wave | 7% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate |
| Wi-Fi / NB-IoT | 3% | Stable | LOW | Power-hungry; unsuitable for battery-only devices |

**Museum note:** This is the primary architecture distance driver (score = 3 in market discovery). Museum security systems (CCTV, intrusion detection) typically run on IP/BACnet/KNX — not EasyWave. LoRaWAN or Zigbee would substantially reduce integration friction for large museum buildings. GfS BUS gateway bridges the gap but adds cost and vendor lock-in.

#### Alternatives at L3 — Battery chemistry

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Alkaline 9V block (standard) | 65% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — default in most variants |
| Lithium 9V block (extended life, cold) | 25% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Preferred for infrequently accessed locations — museums often have secure utility rooms with no regular maintenance access |
| Rechargeable Li-ion with charging circuit | 10% | ^ Emerging | LOW | Analyst estimate — next-gen IoT devices |

**Museum note:** Lithium 9V is strongly preferred for museums with restricted-access utility rooms or conservation-climate storage areas where maintenance visits are infrequent (<1×/year). Longer battery life directly reduces operational burden.

---

### L4-ENC — Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem

```
L4  Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem
 ├── L3  Guard Housing Assembly
 │    ├── L2  Outer casing
 │    │    ├── L1  Front cover (injection molded)
 │    │    ├── L1  Back panel
 │    │    └── L0  ABS/PC blend (standard) or PA6-GF (structural)
 │    └── L2  Safety colour finish
 │         └── L0  RAL 6029 in-mold pigment or spray lacquer
 └── L3  Mounting Hardware Assembly
      ├── L2  Door handle clamp set
      │    ├── L1  Clamp bracket
      │    ├── L1  Fixing screws
      │    └── L0  Stainless steel A4 (preferred for heritage: no corrosion staining)
      └── L2  Glass-frame mounting plate (variant accessory)
           ├── L1  Adapter plate
           └── L0  Stainless steel or anodized aluminium
```

#### Alternatives at L4-ENC level — Outer enclosure material

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS / ABS-PC blend | 55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Standard for injection-molded security devices |
| Glass-filled PA6 (PA6-GF30) | 25% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Structural rigidity, dimensional stability |
| Zinc die-cast | 15% | Stable | MEDIUM | Premium mechanical feel; heavier; traditional hardware aesthetics |
| Die-cast aluminium | 5% | Stable | LOW | IP-rated outdoor variants |

**Museum note:** Zinc die-cast or brushed aluminium enclosures could address heritage aesthetic requirements (natural metal finish vs. green ABS). This is a product-line adaptation opportunity for the museums segment.

#### Alternatives at L0 — Mounting screw material

| Alternative | Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zinc-plated steel | 50% | Stable | MEDIUM | Standard hardware |
| Stainless steel A2 | 35% | ^ Growing | MEDIUM | Corrosion resistance for outdoor / IP45 variant |
| Stainless steel A4 | 10% | ^ Growing | LOW | Marine / heritage preference — no galvanic staining on historic metal fixtures |
| Nylon/polymer screws | 5% | Stable | LOW | Non-conductive; museum display cases |

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Mapping: Wireless Radio Module
sections.content:
**Component category: RF Radio Transmitter Module**

| BOM Position | Function | Safety-critical? | Key alternatives |
|---|---|---|---|
| L4-IOT > L3 RF Transmitter > L2 RF Circuit Board | Broadcasts guard state (open/alarm/battery) to receiver | NO — supervisory layer only; mechanical alarm is safety-critical | 868 MHz proprietary, Zigbee, BLE, LoRaWAN |

**Insight:** The radio module faces fundamentally different competitors depending on the deployment context:
- In **standalone retrofit** deployments: GfS EasyWave receiver ecosystem is sufficient → proprietary protocol wins on simplicity
- In **museum BMS-integrated** deployments: EasyWave creates vendor lock-in friction → Zigbee/BACnet-native modules would win → the current product loses unless the GfS BUS gateway is included at +cost

This is the single component category that most determines fit for NAICS 712110. A LoRaWAN or Zigbee radio variant of the Türwächter would close the architecture distance from 3 → 1.

---

sections.title: Museum-Specific BOM Gap Analysis
sections.content:
| BOM Level | Current state | Gap for museums | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| L3 RF protocol | EasyWave (proprietary) | Museum security systems expect IP/BACnet/KNX | HIGH — primary distance driver |
| L0 Outer casing finish | RAL 6029 safety green | Heritage interiors often require neutral / matching tones | MEDIUM — addressable with colour variant |
| L0 Mounting hardware | Zinc-plated screws | Heritage fixtures require non-staining A4 stainless or non-metallic fixings | MEDIUM — addressable with BOM swap |
| L3 Battery chemistry | Alkaline 9V standard | Museum utility rooms have infrequent maintenance → lithium preferred | LOW — addressable with SKU selection |
| L3 Key override | Profile half-cylinder | Museums often run RFID staff cards → RFID override preferred | LOW — product development opportunity |

---

sections.title: Quality Checks
sections.content:
| Check | Status |
|---|---|
| Every L4 subsystem decomposes to at least L2 | PASS |
| Alternatives present at every design-choice level | PASS |
| All alternative sets sum to 100% ± 1% | PASS |
| Confidence + source on every alternative set | PASS |
| No company names or brand names at any BOM level | PASS — "EasyWave" cited only in protocol comparison (technology class, not brand) |
| Packaging/mounting subsystem included | PASS — L4-ENC |
| Control/interface subsystem included | PASS — L4-IOT |
| BOM vs. VN test: all items end up IN the product | PASS — receiver, repeaters, BMS are excluded (they are VN nodes) |

---

rows.Value: 712110 — Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions
**YES** — product is a manufactured physical device with compositional hierarchy
Service sector (71); BOM is of the product, not the museum's operations

rows.Function: Physically blocks handle depression; releases on 45° guard swing; triggers alarm linkage
rows.Museum relevance: Primary function; must not damage historic door hardware

rows.Function: Generates 95 dB alarm on guard activation; operates without electrical power
rows.Museum relevance: No power dependency critical for heritage buildings with unreliable power infrastructure

rows.Function: Wireless state broadcast (guard open / alarm / battery low) to receiver; battery power management
rows.Museum relevance: Protocol lock-in (EasyWave) creates integration friction with museum security/CCTV systems

rows.Function: Outer housing; surface-mount bracket; installation hardware
rows.Museum relevance: Non-damaging mount to heritage door fixtures; colour/finish may need to match interior aesthetics

Electronic exit control with panic release (wired)

rows.Source: Analyst estimate — standard in EU security hardware
[Straits Research — Buzzer Market](https://straitsresearch.com/report/buzzer-market); dominant for low-power IoT/security
Legacy — higher power draw, moving parts
Analyst estimate — used in premium multi-tone devices
Analyst estimate — large EU installed base; no new platform growth

Preferred for infrequently accessed locations — museums often have secure utility rooms with no regular maintenance access
Premium mechanical feel; heavier; traditional hardware aesthetics
Corrosion resistance for outdoor / IP45 variant
Marine / heritage preference — no galvanic staining on historic metal fixtures

Museum utility rooms have infrequent maintenance → lithium preferred

entities.name: Mechanical Locking Subsystem
entities.description: Physically blocks handle depression; 45° guard swing simultaneously releases handle and triggers alarm via mechanical linkage; key override for authorized bypass

entities.name: Acoustic Alarm Subsystem
entities.description: Generates 95 dB audible alarm on guard activation; piezoelectric mechanism operates without electrical power; optional pre-alarm vibration sensing

entities.name: IoT Monitoring Subsystem
entities.description: 868 MHz radio transmitter broadcasts guard state and alarm events to external receivers; battery-powered; state detection via reed switch

entities.name: Enclosure & Mounting Subsystem
entities.description: Injection-molded outer housing (RAL 6029); surface-mount clamp bracket; fixing hardware; glass-frame adapter variant

entities.name: Guard Actuator Assembly
entities.description: Guard body, pivot axle, return torsion spring, trigger linkage

entities.name: Handle Engagement Mechanism
entities.description: Clamp block that interfaces with door handle; cam element that transfers guard rotation to alarm trigger

entities.name: Key Override Assembly
entities.description: Profile half-cylinder lock housing and bitting mechanism enabling authorized bypass without alarm

entities.name: Piezoelectric Alarm Module
entities.description: PZT piezo transducer on brass diaphragm; ABS resonator housing; volume-adjustable; no electrical dependency for basic alarm function

entities.name: RF Transmitter Module
entities.description: 868 MHz RF circuit board with transceiver IC, crystal oscillator, and PCB trace antenna; EasyWave protocol

entities.name: Power Management Module
entities.description: 9V battery holder with phosphor-bronze spring contacts; LDO voltage regulator; decoupling capacitors

entities.name: State Detection Module
entities.description: Reed switch or Hall-effect sensor detecting guard open/closed position; LED status indicator

entities.name: Guard Housing Assembly
entities.description: ABS/PC outer casing (front + back panel) with RAL 6029 safety green finish

entities.name: Mounting Hardware Assembly
entities.description: Door handle clamp set; fixing screws (zinc-plated steel standard, A4 stainless for outdoor/heritage); glass-frame mounting plate accessory

entities.name: Guard-over-handle (Türwächter-style)
entities.source: Analyst estimate — dominant EU retrofit category

entities.name: Electronic exit control with panic release (wired)
Piezoelectric buzzer
entities.source: Straits Research — Buzzer Market 2024; dominant in low-power IoT/security devices

entities.name: Electromagnetic buzzer
entities.source: Analyst estimate; higher power draw, moving parts

entities.name: Proprietary 868 MHz (EasyWave-class)
entities.source: IHS Markit Connectivity Technology report; large EU installed base, no new platform growth

entities.name: Wi-Fi / NB-IoT
entities.source: Analyst estimate — unsuitable for battery-only devices

entities.name: Lithium 9V block
entities.source: Analyst estimate — preferred for remote/infrequent-access deployments

entities.name: ABS / ABS-PC blend
entities.source: Analyst estimate — standard injection-molded security device enclosure

entities.name: Glass-filled PA6 (PA6-GF30)
entities.source: Analyst estimate — growing structural polymer use in hardware

entities.name: Zinc die-cast
entities.source: Analyst estimate — premium mechanical hardware tradition

entities.bom_position: L4-IOT > L3 RF Transmitter Module > L2 RF Circuit Board Assembly
entities.function: Broadcasts guard state (open/alarm/battery-low) to external receiver units
entities.safety_note: Supervisory only — mechanical alarm operates without electrical power. Radio failure does not impede escape.
entities.market_insight: Museum security platforms expect IP/BACnet/KNX integration. EasyWave requires GfS-specific BUS gateway. A Zigbee or LoRaWAN radio variant closes architecture distance from 3→1 for NAICS 712110.
